title: A ball thrown straight up
scenario: A ball leaves your hand at 20 m/s straight up, from a height of 1.5 m. How high does it go, when does it land, and how fast is it moving when it hits the ground?

assumptions.claim: No air resistance (the ball is in free fall under gravity alone).
g = -10 m/s^2, a clean-arithmetic simplification of -9.81 m/s^2.
The ball is a point mass; its size and spin are ignored.

steps.label: Assert the constant-acceleration relations
steps.latex: v = v_0 + a t \qquad x = x_0 + v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2
steps.prose: These are the formulas the algebra-based course memorizes. (They are quadratures — we will see them fall out of the calculus.)

steps.label: Apex: the top is where velocity is zero
steps.prose: Velocity is zero only for an instant — but acceleration is not. The ball is still being pulled down at g.

steps.label: Maximum height from the timeless equation
steps.prose: Set v = 0 in v² = v₀² + 2a(x − x₀). No time needed — this is the equation an algebra student is handed without its derivation.

steps.label: Time of flight: solve for when it returns to the ground
steps.latex: x_0 + v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2 = 0 \;\implies\; t_{\text{flight}} = \frac{- v_{0} - \sqrt{- 2 a x_{0} + v_{0}^{2}}}{a}
steps.prose: The physical (later) root of the quadratic — the moment it lands.

steps.label: Impact velocity at the ground
steps.prose: Negative because it is moving downward at impact (up is positive).

steps.label: Start from the acceleration
steps.prose: Constant — the only force acting is gravity. On the stacked graph this is a flat line.

steps.label: Velocity is the integral of acceleration
steps.latex: v(t) = \int a \, dt = v_0 + a t
steps.prose: The constant of integration is v₀ (the velocity at t = 0). The slope of the v–t graph is a; the area under the a–t graph is the change in v.

steps.label: Position is the integral of velocity
steps.latex: x(t) = \int v \, dt = \int (v_0 + a t)\, dt = x_0 + v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2
steps.prose: The constant of integration is x₀. The slope of the x–t graph is v; the area under the v–t graph is the change in x.

steps.label: The algebra formula has emerged
steps.prose: This is exactly the formula the algebra course memorizes — here it is the integral with the integrand (a) frozen to a constant. Freeze the integrand and the integral becomes multiply-and-add. That is the algebra.

proof.heading: The algebra and the calculus agree — proven, not asserted.
proof.detail: simplify(algebra - calculus) == 0 for each identity below

checks.claim: The algebra formula $v = v_0 + at$ is the integral $\int a\,dt$ with $v(0)=v_0$.
The algebra formula $x = x_0 + v_0 t + \tfrac12 a t^2$ is the integral $\int v\,dt$ with $x(0)=x_0$.
Velocity is zero at the apex time $t_{\text{apex}}$ — so the apex time is correct.
Max height from the timeless equation equals the calculus trajectory at its apex.
At the flight time the calculus trajectory returns to the ground — so the flight time is correct.
Impact velocity from the timeless equation equals the calculus velocity at landing.

misconception.claim: At the top of the trajectory, the acceleration is zero (the ball 'stops').
misconception.refuted_by: On the v-t graph the velocity passes through zero at the apex, but the line never bends: its slope is $a = -10$ m/s² the whole way. $v = 0$ for an instant; $a$ is never zero.

sign_analysis.rule: speeding up $\iff \operatorname{sign}(v\cdot a) > 0$;  slowing down $\iff \operatorname{sign}(v\cdot a) < 0$

practice.asks: the maximum height above the ground
practice.prompt: The ball leaves your hand at $v_0 = 20$ m/s from a height of $1.5$ m. What is the greatest height it reaches above the *ground*?

choices.misconception: Found only the height *gained* above your hand, $v_0^2/2|g|$, and forgot to add the $1.5$ m release height. The question asks for the height above the ground.
Dropped the factor of $\tfrac12$. Setting $v^2 = v_0^2 - 2|g|h$ to zero gives $h = v_0^2/2|g|$ — the $\tfrac12$ is exactly the one that appears when you integrate $v\,dv$.

algebra_steps.prose: These are the formulas the algebra-based course memorizes. (They are quadratures — we will see them fall out of the calculus.)

practice.asks: the time to reach the apex
practice.prompt: At what time does the ball reach the top of its flight?

choices.misconception: That is the *round-trip* time (up and back). The apex is reached halfway up, at $t = v_0/|g|$ — half as long.
Mishandled the sign: $t = -v_0/a$ with $a = -10$ gives $+2$ s, not $-2$ s. A time cannot be negative.
